feedback.text: fresh about games and honesty Lieve makers, listen, I speak with soft seines today. A gamer, loyalty, with heart and soul, who plays from passion, not for the gain. There were dreams, big and wild, of worlds beautiful and unstitched. Like Anthem, full of promise, but left empty without any chance. A game was sold with a large display, but the end left us cold and clean. No more updates, no fixed ground, the servers close, and the dream. And then The Crew, a journey full of wind, with friends racing, free as a child. Until suddenly called a date, everything was poem, stunned. We bought time, or we believed it, but we rented pixels, quiet and shooting. No disc, no right, no perpetuity, the game disappeared, it was so far. What remains about, ask me mild, from years of play and long night? When possession is only appearance, empty, and digital possession proved so fragile? Is it fair now? Is it just? The signature feels bitter, unfavourable. Say, best people of the law, do you prejudge the gambler? Protect us small, but dedicated, from this digital time trap and regret. ‘Stopping a game’ is more than it is a loss of what you had. A piece of art, a world fine, closed as a quiet domain. I therefore ask, very courtesy, whether you might wish to write to you in the Digital Fairness Act if you know: these games should not simply disappear. If something stops, we know why, and who paid, does not end. Perhaps a duty to play offline, or money back, or something formally. Because we, the players, young and old, who dream, build, digitally familiar, also deserve a little right that not every game is denied to us. Thank you for your time, your open ear, I hope this will affect you very much. For fair games, this is fine, for creator, gamer, big and small.
